coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of Z-scheme Bi7O9I3/g-C3N4 heterojunction modified by carbon quantum dots for synchronous photocatalytic removal of Cr (Ⅵ) and organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Lingli
Shen, Dekui
Zhang, Huiyan
Luo, Kai Hong
Li, Chong
coredata.dc:description: Chromium(VI) (Cr(VI)), a highly toxic metal ion, generally co-exists with organic pollutants in industrial effluents. The clean and effective technology for water purification is an imperative issue but still a challenging task. A series of Bi7O9I3/g-C3N4 (BOI/CN) composites modified by lignin-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were fabricated by hydrothermal method and applied for synchronous photocatalytic removal of Cr (Ⅵ) and levofloxacin (LEV). With the modification of CQDs in BOI/CN heterojunction, the 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN photocatalyst (0.5% content of CQDs) exhibited stronger light-harvesting capacity, more efficient charge separation, and faster electron transfer. Compared to those of BOI (51.2%), CN (36.8%), and BOI/CN (74.4%), the photoreduction efficiency of Cr(VI) reached up to 100% by 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN under 60 min of light irradiation, together with 94.8% degradation efficiency of LEV. The degradation of LEV was dominantly controlled by active species (•OH and •O2 −) identified by electron paramagnetic resonance analysis and free radical trapping experiments. The intermediates of LEV were determined by LC-MS and the possible degradation pathway was speculated in combination with density functional theory calculation, involving defluorination, decarboxylation, quinolone rings opening, and piperazine moieties oxidation reactions. This work provides an advanced strategy for the fabrication of high-efficiency CQDs-based Z-scheme photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of Z-scheme Bi7O9I3/g-C3N4 heterojunction modified by carbon quantum dots for synchronous photocatalytic removal of Cr (Ⅵ) and organic pollutants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chromium(VI) (Cr(VI)), a highly toxic metal ion, generally co-exists with organic pollutants in industrial effluents. The clean and effective technology for water purification is an imperative issue but still a challenging task. A series of Bi7O9I3/g-C3N4 (BOI/CN) composites modified by lignin-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were fabricated by hydrothermal method and applied for synchronous photocatalytic removal of Cr (Ⅵ) and levofloxacin (LEV). With the modification of CQDs in BOI/CN heterojunction, the 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN photocatalyst (0.5% content of CQDs) exhibited stronger light-harvesting capacity, more efficient charge separation, and faster electron transfer. Compared to those of BOI (51.2%), CN (36.8%), and BOI/CN (74.4%), the photoreduction efficiency of Cr(VI) reached up to 100% by 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN under 60 min of light irradiation, together with 94.8% degradation efficiency of LEV. The degradation of LEV was dominantly controlled by active species (•OH and •O2 −) identified by electron paramagnetic resonance analysis and free radical trapping experiments. The intermediates of LEV were determined by LC-MS and the possible degradation pathway was speculated in combination with density functional theory calculation, involving defluorination, decarboxylation, quinolone rings opening, and piperazine moieties oxidation reactions. This work provides an advanced strategy for the fabrication of high-efficiency CQDs-based Z-scheme photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial effluents containing toxic heavy metal ions and organic chemical compounds from electroplating, metal finishing, leather tanning, and steel fabricating pose a growing health risk and ecological threat [1,2]. Among them, soluble hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) and residual antibiotics are of particular environmental concern owing to their mobility, carcinogenicity, and teratogenicity on the ecological cycle chain [3,4]. The traditional treatments including chemical precipitation, electrocatalysis, membrane separation, ion exchange, adsorption, and bioremediation, suffer from the tedious operation, secondary pollution, low efficiency, and high cost [27,5,6,61]. Recently, photocatalysis has emerged as a sustainable and promising purification technology due to its clean, green, and convenient advantages [7,18]. For the effective removal of heavy metal and organic pollutants, it is of great significance to develop desirable photocatalysts with strong light harvesting capacity and photocatalytic activity.

In a variety of photocatalytic materials (TiO2, C3N4, Fe2O3, ZnO, WO3, etc.) [10–13,30,46,8,9], bismuth-based semiconductors like bismuth oxide, bismuthate, and bismuth halide oxide are emerging as a hot topic of research due to tunable band structure and abundant crystal forms [14,15,48]. Among them, Bi7O9I3 (BOI) possesses the unique structure of interchanging halogen atomic layer and [Bi2O2]2+ layer, exhibiting rapid charge transfer and strong visible light absorption ability [16]. However, the pure BOI generally suffers from photocorrosion and electron-hole pairs recombination, leading to a low photocatalytic efficiency [14,17,48]. The construction of BOI-based heterojunction structure has been verified to be an effective strategy to solve these issues. The energy bands of different heterojunction materials are arranged interleaved so that the separation of electron-hole pairs is improved through the internal electrical field, band buckling, and electrostatic effects [18,19,7]. The suitable heterojunction structure could broaden the sunlight response range, and promote electron-hole pairs separation, thus improving the photocatalytic activity [20,21]. It is critical to select a semiconductor that matches the BOI band to construct an effective heterojunction photocatalyst. Even so, the recombination effect in the heterojunction structure might still occur when electrons are clustered at the band edge positions [22]. Pt, Au, and Ag are commonly introduced as a mediator in the heterojunction system to guide the electrons to the surface photocatalyst [23,24]. However, noble metals are expensive and rare, limiting their large-scale applications.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been employed as an ideal alternative to modify the heterojunction due to their chemical inertness, excellent electron reservoir, and transfer properties [25–27,6,61]. CQDs can be prepared from many common carbon-containing substances. Particularly, the green synthesis of CQDs using renewable biomass has the merits of non-toxicity, low cost, and environmental friendliness, which is a win-win strategy for the rational disposal and value-added utilization of solid waste [28,29,33,35]. Among them, lignin, composed of phenyl skeletons and oxygen-containing branches, is the largest natural biomass source of aromatics on the earth. Lignin-derived CQDs possess an amorphous nanocrystal core composed mainly of sp2 conjugated structure and are enriched with oxygen in the form of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which contributes to the superior electronic storage capacity of CQDs. Meanwhile, the unique up- and down-conversion photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs would broaden the absorption region of sunlight and boost the utilization of solar energy [30–32,39,46,53,9]. Many attempts have been made on the CQDs-modified photocatalysts and satisfactory outcomes have been achieved, such as CQDs/BiOBr/CeO2 [15], CQDs/Bi2WO6/g-C3N4 [16], CQDs/BiVO4/Ag3PO4 [7,18], CQDs/BiVO4/Bi3TaO7 [23], etc. Notably, biomass-derived CQDs are rarely utilized in heterojunction systems, and the insightful mechanism of CQDs impacts needs to be further clarified.

In this work, the Bi7O9I3/g-C3N4 (BOI/CN) heterojunction modified by different contents of lignin-derived CQDs were prepared via a facile hydrothermal method. The morphology, structure, chemical composition, and optical and photoelectrochemical properties of the prepared photocatalysts were thoroughly investigated. The photocatalytic activity was estimated through synchronous photoreduction of Cr (Ⅵ) and degradation of levofloxacin (LEV) under simulated light irradiation. The photocatalytic mechanism was elucidated by the free radical trapping experiment and electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR) analysis. The photodegradation pathways of LEV were proposed according to the analysis of liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC−MS) combined with density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
The materials and chemicals were detailed in Text S1.
As illustrated in Scheme 1, CQDs were synthesized through a simple method (Text S2) according to previous works [29,33–35].

The g-C3N4 (CN) and CQDs/g-C3N4 (CQD/CN) were synthesized through thermal polymerization from melamine. 0.025 g of CQDs and 5 g of C3H6O6 were dispersed in an ethanol solution and sonicated for 0.5 h. The mixed solution was vacuum dried at 60 °C and ground uniformly into a crucible. It was subsequently subjected to heating in a muffle furnace at 5 °C/min to 550 ℃ and maintained for 4 h. Naturally cooled to ambient temperature, the solid product was ground into uniform powder in a crucible and noted as CQD/CN. The CB powder was obtained by heating C3H6O6 directly in the muffle furnace without the addition of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Bi7O9I3 and Bi7O9I3-based composites
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of Bi7O9I3 (BOI) and BOI-based composites were fabricated via a hydrothermal method. 4.368 g of Bi(NO3)3⋅5 H2O was dispersed into 120 mL EG and sonicated for 30 min to get solution A. 1.494 g of KI, 0.0828 g of CN, and a designed amount of CQDs was taken into 120 mL ethyl alcohol (EG) with sonicating for 30 min to get solution B. Afterwards, solution A was manually mixed into solution B with stirring for 2 h. The obtained solution was then treated in a 300 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 160 ℃ for 24 h. The resulting solution was centrifugalized at high speed and cleaned four times with DI water. The CQD/BOI/CN powers were acquired through vacuum drying at 70 °C for 12 h. The contents of CQDs to Bi7O9I3/g-C3N4 (BOI/CN) composite were controlled at 0.2 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 1 wt%, which were denoted as 0.2-CQD/BOI/CN, 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN, and 1.0-CQD/BOI/CN, respectively. The pure BOI, BOI/CN, and CQDs/Bi7O9I3 (CQD/BOI) were obtained using the above similar procedure in the absence of CN and CQDs, CQDs, CN, respectively.
The characterization of samples was detailed in Text S3, and photoelectrochemical measurement in Text S4.

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized photocatalysts was evaluated by photoreduction of Cr(VI) and degradation of LEV under 300 W Xe lamp irradiation (100 mW/cm2, CEL-PF300-T6). As in each test, 50 mg of the photocatalyst was added to 100 mL of aqueous solution with Cr (Ⅵ) (20 mg L−1) and LEV solution (20 mg L−1). The mixed solution was next stirred continuously in the dark for 30 min. Afterward, the solution was irradiated at 25 ℃ for 60 min. In the reaction process, 3 mL of the reactive solution was taken every 10 min and filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane. The concentration of Cr(VI) in the supernatant was determined via the standard diphenylcarbazide (DPC) method (Text S5). The concentration of LEV was recorded at λ max = 279 nm using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer.

The photostability of the as-prepared photocatalysts was carried out through a series of consecutive recycling experiments. The spent samples were re-collected by high-speed centrifugation, cleaned several times with deionized water, and dried at 80 °C for 12 h before recycling. In addition, XRD and FT-IR analyses were also conducted for the used materials to elucidate the photocatalytic activity loss during the recycling.

The EPR spectra were measured on a Bruker EMXplus-6/1 to identify the active species. 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, 50 μL) was used as a spin capture reagent. The active species were identified indirectly by free radical scavenging experiments with the addition of reactive species scavenger in the photocatalytic process. The typical scavengers employed were N2 for the dissolved O2, 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ, 1.0 mM) for the superoxide radical (•O2 -), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA-2Na, 10 mM) for the holes (h+), isopropanol (IPA, 10 mM) for the hydroxyl radical (•OH), respectively.

The morphology of the fabricated photocatalysts was observed by SEM. In Fig. 1a, the pure BOI exhibits stacked 3D layered flower-like microspheres ranging from approximately 1 to 2 µm in diameter. This unique morphology facilitates the transfer of electrons and the adsorption of contaminants. In Fig. 1b, the CN sample possesses a plicated lamellar structure and would tend to stack and agglomerate due to high-temperature polymerization. In terms of BOI/CN (Fig. 1c) and CQD/BOI (Fig. 1d), CN and CQDs are irregularly scattered on the surface of BOI spheres. The smoothness of the composite surface is substantially lower than that of the pure BOI. The CQD/CN (Fig. 1e) and CN have almost identical bulk morphology. In Fig. 1f, the surface of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN is coated with CN and CQDs, resulting in a more curved and rough surface. This indicates that nanocomposites have been successfully prepared.

The TEM and HR-TEM images further revealed the internal structure and lattice of the photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 2a, the lignin-derived CQDs are well-dispersed, 0-dimensional quasi-spherical spheres with sizes less than 10 nm. The lattice spacing of CQDs in the inset is around 0.32 nm corresponding to the (0 0 2) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon. Fast Fourier transform (FFT) image obtained from the HR-TEM image confirms the presence of hexagonal diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic C in the inner regions of CQDs. The TEM image of BOI in Fig. 2b presents a distinct flower-like nanoflake, and the pristine CN in Fig. 2c possesses an amorphous structure. The TEM image of CQD/BOI in Fig. 2d exhibit that the small-sized CQDs are attached to the surface of the BOI nanoflowers with no change in the size and morphology. For 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN nanocomposite (Fig. 2e), the structures of CN and BOI are coupled together, and CQDs nanoparticles are attached to the surface (Fig. 2f). The close contact between CQDs, CN, and BOI contributes to charge transfer during the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, the TEM mapping (Fig. 2g-n) shows that 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN nanocomposite contains C, N, O, Cl, I, and Bi, all of which could be apparently detected. The detailed content of each element is listed in Table S1, where the content of C, N, and Cl increases with the proportion of CQDs modifications. These outcomes further confirm that ternary CQD/BOI/CN/ photocatalysts are successfully prepared.

The crystal features of the fabricated photocatalysts were characterized by XRD. In Fig. 3a, within the range of 10–80°, CQDs show a broad peak centered at 26.7°, which is ascribed to (0 0 2) planes of graphitic carbon. The pure BOI has distinct characteristic peaks at 29.2°, 32.2°, 46.1°, and 55.6°, which are attributed to the (1 0 2), (1 1 0), (2 0 0), and (2 1 2) crystal planes (JCPDS 40–0548) [14,48], respectively. The pure CN possesses a weak diffraction peak at 13.2° and another sharp peak around 27.6°, which denotes the (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) plane of aromatic compounds and the stacked conjugated aromatic systems (JCPDS 87–1526), respectively. For BOI/CN, CQD/BOI, and CQD/CN, the characteristic peaks of BOI, and CN in the binary composites can be apparently seen, while the flat peak of CQDs in the nanocomposites is very insignificant due to its low level. The ternary composite CQD/BOI/CN exhibits distinctive three-part characteristic peaks belonging to BOI, CN, and CQDs, respectively. As the CQDs content increases from 0.2% to 1.0%, the (0 0 2) lattice plane peak is strengthened, suggesting more coupling of the CQDs to the BOI and CN [29,33,35]. It is noteworthy that the characteristic plane (100) of CN cannot be clearly observed in both BOI/CN and CQD/BOI/CN due to the comparatively too strong intensity of the characteristic peaks of BOI. Fig. 3b showed FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared photocatalysts for comparison. The broad characteristic absorption band at 3440–3280 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of O-H for CQDs. The stretching vibration band at 3280–3087, 1766, 1603, 1399, and 1122 cm−1 are related to N-H, CO, CC, C-O, and C-N bonds [29,33–35]. For pure BOI, the vibration bands at 3420, 1593, 1380, 1066, 881, and 507 cm−1 are characteristic of O-H, H-O-H bonds of adsorbed H2O, I-O-I, Bi-O, and I-O bonds, respectively [19,23]. The CN exhibits a broad peak at 3600–3000 cm−1, which is assigned to the N-H bond originating from incompletely condensed N-H bonds or residual hydrogen atoms bound to the edges of graphite-like CN. The stretching vibration bands at 1670–1122 and 804 cm−1 belong to CN and C-N bonds [36]. Similar characteristic bands are well traced in FT-IR spectra of BOI/CN, CQD/BOI, CQD/CN, and CQD/BOI/CN, which further proves that the successful preparation of the binary and ternary nanocomposites. In comparison, the intensity of the stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups in CQD/BOI/CN is gradually strengthened with the increase of CQDs content.

XPS spectra were adopted to analyze the chemical compositions on the surface of the photocatalysts. Fig. 4a demonstrates the survey spectra including C 1 s, N 1 s, Cl 2p, Bi 4 f, O 1 s, and I 3d of the as-prepared photocatalysts. The C 1 s spectra in Fig. 4b can be divided into characteristic peaks at 288.7 eV (CO), 286.1 eV (C-O/C-N), 284.8 eV (C-C/CC) for CQDs, and 288.0 eV (N-CN), 284.8 eV (C-C/CC) for CN [37,38]. The N 1 s spectra in Fig. 4c show three peaks of C-N-H, N-(C)3, and CN-C at 401.6, 400.2, and 398.8 eV for CQDs and CN [31,39]. The interaction of the N atoms in CQD/BOI/CN and certain species through chemical bonding or intense electrostatic gravitational forces may cause a shift in the position of the N 1 s peak. The shift in binding energy implies the transfer of electrons from BOI to CN and CQDs at the interface [14,48]. The Cl 2p spectra in Fig. 4d are fitted into two peaks of Cl 2p3/2, Cl 2p1/2 at 199.6 and 198.2 eV originating from CQDs. The Cl 2p peaks of CQDs-based nanocomposites are unapparent on account of the low content of CQDs. The Bi 4 f spectra in Fig. 4e display two peaks at 163.9 eV (Bi 4 f5/2) and 158.9 eV (Bi 4 f7/2) for BOI-based nanocomposites [15]. The O 1 s spectra in Fig. 4f are fitted into two peaks at 532.9 eV (CO) and 531.4 eV (C-O) for CQDs, and 531.8 eV (surface chemisorbed H2O/OH-), 530.3 eV (Bi-O in [Bi2O2]2+ slabs) for BOI [14,40,41,48,56]. The I 3d spectra in Fig. 4g display two obvious peaks at 630.4 eV (I 3d3/2) and 618.8 eV (I 3d5/2) in BOI-based nanocomposites, indicating that the valence of I in BOI is − 1 [14,17,48]. The above findings prove the interconnection between CQDs, BOI, and CN structures in the ternary nanocomposites, which benefits the electron transfer and improves the photocatalytic performance.

The actual color of all the photocatalysts can be seen in Fig. 5a, the modified nanocomposites are darker than the pristine photocatalysts. Particularly, the color of the CQD/BOI/CN nanocomposites deepened as the CQDs content varied from 0% to 1.0%. UV–vis DRS spectra in Fig. 5b display that CQDs have an absorption edge of ∼650 nm and two characteristic peaks at 261.4 and 489.9 nm, which are corresponding to π-π * transition of sp2 conjugated structure and n-π * transitions of heteroatom doping defects, respectively [42]. The absorption boundaries of BOI, BOI/CN, CQD/BOI, and CQD/BOI/CN are close to each other around 605.0–656.8 nm, while that of CN and CQD/CN are relatively narrow at 467.2 and 483.4 nm, limiting their photocatalytic efficiency. CQD/BOI possesses the broadest light absorption range, and all BOI-based nanocomposites have a satisfactory visible light response. Compared to BOI, CN, and BOI/CN, CQD/BOI, CQD/CN, and CQD/BOI/CN composites display the strengthened light-harvesting capacity with the addition of CQDs, especially in response to ultraviolet and near-infrared light. The CQD/BOI/CN has a slight blue shift with increasing CQDs content and the absorption intensity is mildly diminished. It is probably due to the interfacial interactions that hinder the light absorption. Nevertheless, the light absorption capacity is not the exclusive factor influencing the photocatalytic activity. The energy bandgaps (E g) of photocatalysts are calculated by Eq. (1) [43]:

Where α, hν, and A are absorption coefficients, photon energy, and constant, respectively. The E g is estimated by extrapolation of a plot of (αhv)1/2 versus hν as displayed in Fig. 5c. The E g of BOI, CN, CQD/BOI, and CQD/CN are calculated to be 1.67, 2.39, 1.61, and 2.27 eV, suggesting a narrowing of E g after the introduction of CQDs.

The PL emission behavior of CQDs was explored under different excitation wavelengths. In Fig. 5d, when excited by the short-wavelength light from 400 to 600 nm, CQDs present a slight red shift from long-wavelength emission of 542–600 nm. This down-conversion PL of CQDs can extend multiple photons to the near-infrared region, thus improving the utilization of sunlight and achieving a full spectrum response. The PL spectra of the fabricated photocatalysts in Fig. 5e show that the 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN has the most pronounced fluorescence quenching, revealing the lowest recombination rate of photogenerated carriers [30,44,46,9]. Besides, the PL intensity of CQD/BOI, CQD/CN, and CQD/BOI/CN is weaker than that of the pure BOI, CN, and BOI/CN. The results indicate that the introduction of CQDs enables to inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers. In Fig. 5f, all the photocatalysts have regular photocurrent responses when the light is turned on or off. Their photocurrent intensity is in the order of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN > 1.0-CQD/BOI/CN > 0.2-CQD/BOI/CN > BOI/CN > CQD/BOI > CQD/CN >BOI >CN > CQDs. The introduction of conductive CQDs to BOI, CN, or BOI/CN heterojunction considerably increases the electron lifetime and current separation efficiency [30,45,46,9]. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured to further illustrate the interfacial electron transfer rate of the samples (Fig. 5g). Among them, 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN possesses the smallest arc radius of impedance curve, representing the fastest interfacial charge transfer [47]. The constructed CQD/BOI/CN composites with rapid electron transfer rate and effective carriers separation efficiency are qualified to participate in the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, VBXPS spectra (XPS valence band in Fig. S1) were conducted to quantitatively depict the band structure of the as-prepared photocatalysts. The energy of the conduction band (E
CB ) and valence band (E
VB ) position can be determined by Eq. (2).

With the introduction of CQDs, the E
CB of CQD/BOI (−0.84 eV) and CQD/CN (−0.75 eV) show a gradually downward shift from that of BOI (−0.95 eV) and CN (−0.85 eV), respectively. The E
CB level reveals the photoreduction ability of the photocatalysts (Fig. 5h) [17,37].

The photothermal effect as an assistant factor to facilitate photocatalytic activity involves the transformation of photon energy into thermal energy [24]. The photothermal materials like metal and carbon materials, function as heat emitters at the reaction interface. The thermal energy of materials is in turn dissipated to the surrounding solution. In general, an elevated reaction temperature could lower the apparent activation energy in the photocatalytic system and thereby improve the photocatalytic performance [14,48,49]. Hence, the surface temperature of the prepared photocatalysts was recorded by an infrared thermal imager to quantitatively analyze the photothermal effect. All the samples were subjected to a Xe lamp irradiation at 0, 1, 5, and 15 min for the thermo images (Fig. S2). As illustrated in Fig. 6a-d, the surface temperature of BOI/CN composite significantly increases from ambient temperature (28.5 ℃) to 52.4 ℃ within 1 min of irradiation, and slowly increases to 56.0 ℃ within 15 min of irradiation. While the surface temperature of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN increases sharply from 28.7 to 71.2 ℃ within 1 min of irradiation, and gradually increases to 79.6 ℃ within 15 min of irradiation. In the photocatalytic process, there are two dominant sources of heat: the direct thermal radiation from the light source as well as the intrinsic photothermal effect of the sample. The thermal variations induced by the external light irradiation are constant. The thermal increment of BOI/CN composites can be attributed to the external light source. The sudden increase in thermal energy of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN may be due to the synergy and SPR effect of CQDs and Bi, which contributes to the photothermal effect [14,24,48].

The photocatalytic activity of the fabricated photocatalysts was estimated for synchronous removal of Cr (VI) and LEV under 300 W Xe lamp irradiation. In Fig. 7a, the monomer photocatalysts of CQDs, BOI, and CN exhibit limited reduction efficiency of 4.5%, 51.2%, and 36.8% after 60 min of light irradiation, which may be attributed to the intensive photon scattering and rapid charge recombination. The binary nanocomposites of CQD/BOI, CQD/CN, and BOI/CN present higher reduction efficiency of 64.1%, 41.6%, and 74.4% due to the improved charge separation efficiency. The construction of ternary heterojunctions CQD/BOI/CN can further enhance the photocatalytic performance for the Cr (VI) removal with the optimal reduction efficiency up to 100% (0.5-CQD/BOI/CN). As shown in Fig. S3, the high-resolution XPS spectra of Cr 2p for 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN after adsorption in darkness for 30 min display only the characteristic peak of Cr(VIII) at 579.4 eV. The Cr 2p spectra for 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN after photocatalytic reaction for 30 min have a weak peak at 579.4 eV (Cr(VI)) and a strong peak at 576.6 eV (Cr(VII)), which supplementally verifies that Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr(III). The photocatalytic reduction kinetics for the photocatalysts are fitted with a pseudo-first-order kinetic model [15,50,51]:
(3)
− ln ( C /
C
0
) = kt
Where k is the kinetic reaction rate (min−1). The linear kinetic curves of the Cr(VI) reduction over the photocatalysts are depicted in Fig. 7b. The 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN possesses the highest Cr(VI) reduction rate of 0.08725 min−1, corresponding to 164.3, 9.3,15.2 6.4, 12.7, 4.8, 2.8, and 2.4 times higher than that of CQDs (0.00053 min−1), BOI (0.00939 min−1), CN (0.00575 min−1), CQD/BOI (0.01357 min−1), CQD/CN (0.00689 min−1), BOI/CN (0.01825 min−1), 0.2-CQD/BOI/CN (0.03099 min−1), 1.0-CQD/BOI/CN (0.03671 min−1), respectively. The excessive accumulation of CQDs on the surface of BOI/CN nanocomposite would inevitably hinder the charge transfer and active sites, with consequently lowering the photoreduction efficiency. A loss of 20.6% in photocatalytic activity can be seen in Fig. 7c after five cycles which may be due to the loss of catalyst quantity during filtration, the inevitable photocorrosion of the photocatalysts and the blockage of the reactive site [19,32,52,53]. Additionally, XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN before and after five recycle experiments are measured (Fig. S4). In comparison, there are no emerging peaks and significant shifts appeared, suggesting the invariant crystalline structure and surface functional groups of the reused 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN.

As for the synchronous photocatalytic degradation of LEV (Fig. 7d), all the photocatalysts achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium after constant stirring for 30 min in the darkness. The blank sample has negligible degradation capacity of LEV (20 mg L−1). Comparatively, the binary nanocomposites of CQD/BOI (62.5%), CQD/CN (36.8%), and BOI/CN (74.4%) exhibit higher LEV degradation efficiency than the monomer photocatalysts of CQDs (4.5%), BOI (39.8%), CN (26.8%) after irradiation for 60 min. The constructed ternary 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN heterojunction shows the optimal LEV degradation efficiency of 94.8%, which is in accordance with the optical and photoelectrochemical results. In Fig. 7e, the maximum reaction rate over 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN (0.03863 min−1) is about 72.9, 6.2, 9.9 times higher than the monomer photocatalysts of CQDs (0.00053 min−1), BOI (0.00626 min−1), CN (0.00389 min−1), and 3.1, 6.7, 2.2 times higher than the binary photocatalysts of CQD/BOI (0.01262 min−1), CQD/CN (0.00575 min−1), BOI/CN (0.0174 min−1). It is noteworthy that the introduction of insufficient and excessive CQDs negatively affects the further improvement of photoreaction and degradation efficiency [36,54,55]. The reusability of the photocatalyst is vital for the application in practice, and the five photocatalytic cycling experiments were performed. In Fig. 7f, the degradation efficiency of 0.5- CQD/BOI/CN for LEV decrease to 77.8% after the fifth cyclic experiment. These results manifest the superior recyclability and stability of the as-synthesized photocatalysts. The comparison of the photocatalytic performance of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN and other materials for Cr and LEV removal is summarized in Table S2. The fabricated 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN exhibits superior photocatalytic reduction and degradation performance, with advantages in terms of shorter radiation time, high pollutant concentration, and less catalyst usage.

The initial pH and pollutant concentration are key factors to affect the photocatalytic activity of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN. The effects of initial pH values (3−11) and pollutant concentrations (5–40 mg L−1) on the removal of Cr(VI) and LEV by 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN photocatalyst are displayed in Fig. S5. The increase in pH from 3 to 7 improves the removal efficiency of both Cr(VI) and LEV. However, the removal efficiency sharply decreases at pH 7–11. It indicates that pH 7 is the optimal pH value for the photoreduction of Cr(VI) and LEV, which is consistent with the reported results [15,20]. 100% removal efficiency can be achieved within an irradiation time of 60 min under the low pollutant concentrations of 5–20 mg L−1. As the pollutant concentration increases from 20 to 40 mg L−1, the removal efficiency significantly decrease to 70.7∼77.0%. It can be attributed to the limited active sites on the surface of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN and restrained light transmission in the solution with the high concentration of pollutants.

To gain insight into the photocatalytic reaction mechanism, EPR measurement was performed under simulated sunlight irradiation to determine the free radical species. The characteristic quartets with intensities of 1:2:2:1 and 1:1:1:1 are corresponding to DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 – signals, respectively. In Fig. 8a, b, the pure BOI and BOI/CN composite both exhibit very weak peaks of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 – signals under light irradiation. As the introduction of CQDs, the significant characteristic peaks of the two signals are observed for CQD/BOI nanocomposite (Fig. 8c). In comparison, 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN nanocomposite presents apparently stronger signals of both DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 – (Fig. 8d). The generation process of active species can be clarified through the investigation on the energy band structure. The electrons in the CB of BOI seem to have the potential to reduce O2 to •O2 –, since the ECB (−0.97 eV) position is lower than that of •O2 – (−0.13 eV vs NHE). But the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in pure BOI hinders the generation of free radicals, resulting in poor photocatalytic performance. BOI/CN exhibits a typical type II heterojunction with building a dual transfer mechanism, where electrons migrate from the CB of BOI to CN and holes migrate from the VB of CN to BOI. This traditional model shows the limited generation of active species due to higher E CB of CN than BOI, and lower E VB of BOI than CN. The accepted redox ability of BOI/CN heterojunction is mainly ascribed to efficient electron-hole pairs separation rather than active species. In CQD/BOI nanocomposite, CQDs as an electron reservoir significantly facilitate the efficient separation of photogenerated electron holes. The enriched electrons in the surface of CQDs and the CB of CQD/BOI (−0.84 eV) can be both used for the reduction reaction accompanied by promoting the generation of •O2 –. The VB of CQD/BOI cannot oxidize H2O to •OH as its E VB (0.87 eV) position is lower than that of H2O/•OH (2.38 eV vs NHE). The separated electrons, holes, •O2 – and •OH (from the secondary reaction) are responsible for the strengthened photocatalytic activity of CQD/BOI. As for 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN, the photoexcited electrons migrate from the CB of CN to the VB of BOI via CQDs as a bridge. Then, the electrons in the CB of BOI (−0.97 eV) and holes in the VB of CN (1.54 eV) can be used for photocatalytic reduction and oxidation. The accelerated electronic transfer by CQDs and lower CB position dramatically enhances the generation of •O2 – and •OH from the secondary reaction. Therefore, 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN processes superior photocatalytic performance.

The EPR spectra of different CQD/BOI/CN composites in Fig. 9a manifest that the characteristic peak intensity of DMPO-•OH increases and then decreases as the CQDs content ranges from 0.2% to 1.0% under light irradiation. The situation of DMPO-•O2 − is consistent with that of •OH (Fig. 9b), and the 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN composite shows the optimal intensity of both DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − signals. It is known that the photoexcited •OH and •O2 − radicals are essential for the photocatalytic activity of CQD/BOI/CN composite, whereas excessive or insufficient CQDs content has a negative impact on the generation of active species. It is consistent with the experimental results of the photocatalytic performance evaluation. According to the EPR analysis, the LEV degradation by 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN mainly relied on the h+, •O2 –, •OH (from the secondary reaction of •O2 –), and the electrons are responsible for the Cr(VI) reduction. The active species trapping experiments were implemented to further identify the contribution of each free radical. In Fig. 9c, the addition of IPA, BQ, and EDTA-2Na trapping agents has a minor effect on the photoreduction reaction. 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN with Na2S2O3 added exhibits the lowest removal rate of 38.6%, revealing that e- is the only dominant factor in the photoreduction of Cr (VI). In Fig. 9d, the degradation rate of LEV over 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN is slightly decreased from 99.9% to 70.5%, 47.2%, 36.8%, 41.2% in the presence of IPA (•OH scavenger), the BQ (•O2 – scavenger), EDTA-2Na (h+ scavenger), and N2 (dissolved O2 scavenger). The results illustrate that dissolved O2 can be photoreduced by e- to yield •O2 – radicals for attacking the LEV molecules with •OH and h+, which are the dominant reactive radicals for the LEV degradation.

Accordingly, the potential photocatalytic mechanism of the 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN composite is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 10. The excellent photocatalytic performance of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN may be ascribed to the fabrication of Z-scheme heterojunction. The BOI and CN are photoexcited under the sunlight irradiation, producing electrons and holes at their CBs and VBs, separately. As CQDs process outstanding electrons collect and transfer ability, the electrons in the CB of CN first migrate to the CQDs surface. Then, the electrons on CQDs allow quick recombination with the holes on the VB of BOI. CQDs act as an electron reservoir or buffer to build a bridge between the CB of CN and the VB of BOI. This Z-scheme system can both accelerate the rate of electron transfer and effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs inside BOI and CN. Thus, exceptionally high yields of electrons and holes are formed in the CB of BOI and the VB of CN, which can be directly used in photo-redox reactions. The CB position (−0.97 eV) of BOI is more negative than that of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV vs NHE), while the VB of CN is not more positive than H2O/•OH (2.38 eV vs NHE). The enriched electrons in the CB of BOI can reduce the adsorbed O2 to •O2 − (O2 + e-→ •O2 −), then to •HO2 −, H2O2, and •OH from the secondary reaction (•O2 − + e- + H+→ •HO2 −, •HO2 − + H+→ H2O2, H2O2
↔ 2•OH) [20,41,56]. Noteworthily, the above structural analysis has verified the N, Cl co-doping of CQDs, so the electrons accumulated on the surface of CQDs could reduce Cr(VI) and activate O2 to yield plentiful active species owing to its abundant surface defect sites. The organic pollutants (LEV) can be attacked by these active species (•O2 −, •OH) and holes, eventually oxidized to CO2 and H2O. The electrons, holes, and active species can be sustainably refreshed by the sunlight irradiation, leading to highly efficient photocatalytic redox capability of 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN composite.

DFT calculations (method in Text S6) were employed to explore the charge distribution of organic pollutant. The chemical structure of the LEV molecule is described in Fig. 11a. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) in Fig. 11b represent the ability to lose electron and the deficient-electron sites of the LEV molecule, respectively. Combined with the electrostatic potential (ESP) profile (Fig. 11c) on the molecular surface of LEV, the peripheral regions of N19 and N23 are susceptibly attacked by positive holes, while O11, O13, O14, and F26 tend to be attacked by anionic radicals (•O2 −), promoting of molecular interface reactions [57]. The natural population analysis (NPA) charge distribution and Fukui index of LEV were calculated to quantitatively evaluate reactive sites of electrophilic, nucleophilic, and radical attacking. From Fig. 11d, C8 and O11 in a quinolone ring exhibit the highest f 0 values of 0.0642 and 0.0634. Meanwhile, C9 connected to a carboxyl group, has the high f - and f 0 of 0.0385 and 0.0393. It implies that the quinolone ring is vulnerable to decarboxylation and cleavage by electrophilic •O2 − attack. The high Fukui index values of N19 (f - = 0.0958, f 0 = 0.0534) and N23 (f - = 0.041, f 0 = 0.0216) make piperazine epoxidation feasible. The high f 0 values of 0.0271 and 0.0265 for C11 and F26 are crucial for the defluorination reaction. The f - and f 0 values of C4, C1, C3, and O18 are also high, contributing to the all-cleavage of LEV molecules to small molecules.

To elucidate the photocatalytic oxidation process of the target pollutants, LC−MS was further performed to determine the intermediate products. The major chemical formula, m/z, and possible structure are listed in Fig. S6 and Table S3. On this basis, four feasible photocatalytic degradation pathways of LEV are deduced in Fig. 12, primarily involving defluorination, decarboxylation, quinolone rings cleavage, and piperazine moieties oxidation [57–59]. In Path I, the initial substrate LEV (m/z 362) is attacked by active species (h+, •O2 −, and •OH) and L1 (m/z 360) is formed with the replacement of the F group by a hydroxyl group. The quinolone moieties are further oxidized to product L2 (m/z 392) and then the ring is opened to yield products L3 (m/z 322), L4 (m/z 233), and L5 (m/z 188) [27,6,60,61]. Product L2 could also be decarboxylated and then taken off the piperazine ring to obtain L8 (m/z 290), L9 (m/z 262), and L10 (m/z 232). The second and third degradation paths are the demethylation and decarboxylation from LEV to yield L12 (m/z 348) and L19 (m/z 318), respectively. The subsequent deamination reaction results in the loss of the quinolone ring to obtain L14 (m/z 278) and L15 (m/z 233) [62,63]. The fourth degradation path refers to the piperazine epoxidation and ring cleavage to obtain product L20 (m/z 392). The aldehyde groups of L20 are removed to product L23 (m/z 279) and the F group is simultaneously substituted with a hydroxyl group to product L15 (m/z 233) [64]. Then L15 undergoes the dehydration reaction to product L16 (m/z 217) and the quinolone rings are further destroyed under the continuous oxidation [65]. Ultimately, these intermediates are mineralized to small organic molecules, CO2 and H2O via deep oxidation reaction, and the photocatalytic degradation of the LEV on 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN composite is achieved.

A series of UV–vis-light-driven Z-scheme CQD/BOI/CN composites were successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The optimal composite (0.5-CQD/BOI/CN) features the broadened UV–vis light absorption region, highly effective photogenerated carrier separation, and enhanced redox ability. The photoreduction efficiency of Cr (Ⅵ) is up to 100% over 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN under 60 min of light irradiation, and its reaction rate (0.08725 min−1) is about 4.8 times higher than that of BOI/CN (0.01825 min−1). The synergetic degradation efficiency of LEV reaches 94.8% under 60 min of light irradiation, and the reaction rate (0.03863 min−1) is 2.2 times higher than that of BOI/CN (0.0174 min−1). CQDs sandwiched with BOI/CN heterogeneous interface act as an electron reservoir, facilitating the efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and accelerating the charge transfer. The down-conversion PL property of CQDs is conducive to broadening the UV–vis light absorption region. It was found that LEV degradation was dominantly controlled by the h+, •O2 −, and •OH regarding to the free radical trapping experiments and EPR analysis. According to the DFT calculation and LC-MS analysis, the decomposition pathway of LEV involves the defluorination, decarboxylation, quinolone rings opening, and piperazine moieties oxidation reactions. This work provides a promising approach to fabricating the CQDs-based Z-scheme photocatalysts for synergistic removal of Cr(VI) and organic pollutants for efficient water purification.

Chromium(VI) (Cr(VI)), a highly toxic metal ion, generally co-exists with organic pollutants in industrial effluents. The clean and effective technology for water purification is an imperative issue but still a challenging task. This work provides an advanced strategy for the fabrication of high-efficiency CQD-based Z-scheme photocatalysts for environmental remediation.
